attributes.stem:
A seven-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent with a three-week history of an itchy, scaly patch on his scalp with associated hair loss. He is otherwise well. He has eczema and gluten intolerance, and uses topical steroids frequently. He recently started football training and his parents state a few other children in the class have similar symptoms. His mother has psoriasis and is on immunotherapy.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a 4 cm circular area of scale and alopecia on the vertex of the scalp. There is scaling in the alopecia areas with black dots speckled throughout. There is no lymphadenopathy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Scalp scraping for microscopy and culture
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features suggestive of tinea capitis, a fungal infection of the scalp that commonly affects school-aged children. It is contagious and risk factors include being immunosuppressed, postmenopausal women, sharing contaminated items, playing contact sports, having infected pets and crowded housing. The presence of a scaly patch with associated hair loss and black dots in a scaley hair-free area (broken hair) is characteristic. The diagnosis needs to be confirmed before starting treatment as other conditions can present similarly such as psoriasis.

Scalp scraping for microscopy and culture is the gold standard investigation for diagnosing tinea capitis. Microscopy can provide rapid confirmation of fungal elements, while culture identifies the specific organism (commonly Trichophyton or Microsporum species) though can take up to 4 weeks and guides antifungal therapy. Samples should include scales and broken hair stubs.

Wood's light examination is unreliable for diagnosing tinea capitis as the most common causative organism in many countries, Trichophyton tonsurans, does not fluoresce. Only infections caused by some Microsporum species will show fluorescence thus this is not a reliable test for excluding tinea capitis.

Scalp biopsy is overly invasive as a first-line investigation and is rarely necessary. It might be considered if the diagnosis remains unclear after less invasive investigations or if there is no response to appropriate therapy.

Patch testing is used to identify contact allergens in cases of suspected allergic contact dermatitis. It would not be helpful in diagnosing a fungal infection of the scalp.

Complete blood count would not assist in diagnosing tinea capitis. There are typically no systemic manifestations or characteristic blood count changes associated with this condition.

Once the diagnosis is confirmed, oral antifungal therapy (such as terbinafine or griseofulvin) is required as topical treatments alone cannot penetrate the hair shaft adequately. Treatment usually continues for 6-8 weeks, thorough cleaning should be completed to remove the fomites and all close contacts should be examined for evidence of infection.
learningPoints: Scalp scrapings and fungal culture are essential for diagnosis of tinea capitis as clinical features alone are unreliable.
Wood's light examination is not reliable for diagnosing tinea capitis as most causative organisms do not fluoresce.
references: Oakley A, Bell H, Mitchell G. Tinea capitis. DermNet NZ. 2020. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/tinea-capitis

Hill RC, Gold JAW, Lipner SR. Comprehensive Review of Tinea Capitis in Adults: Epidemiology, Risk Factors, Clinical Presentations, and Management. Journal of Fungi. 2024; 10(5):357.  Available at: https://www.mdpi.com/2309-608X/10/5/357
